hook: You already know that drawing a line through a scatter plot captures a relationship between variables — but how do you know that line is the *best* possible one, and what does 'best' even mean statistically? Classical linear regression answers this precisely, yet the full power of the method only reveals itself when you see it through the lens of linear algebra and probability theory. How can a single unified framework handle everything from a simple two-variable regression to a complex multi-factor experiment?
concept: Every topic in this unit is a different facet of one central idea: modeling the mean of a response variable as a linear combination of predictors and then using probability theory to make rigorous inferences about those relationships — captured formally by the model $\mathbf{y} = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Linear Model in Matrix Form
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to express any linear regression setup compactly using matrix notation, making complex multi-predictor models as easy to write — and manipulate — as a single equation.

Master the theoretical guarantee that the ordinary least squares estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top \mathbf{y}$ is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE) under mild assumptions, so you can defend why OLS is the default choice.

You will derive the exact sampling distributions of the coefficient estimates and error variance under normality, providing the probabilistic foundation needed for hypothesis tests and confidence intervals.
Apply the full regression framework to models with several predictors simultaneously, and learn to interpret coefficients, assess model fit, and perform inference in a multi-variable setting.

You will derive the least squares estimator by minimizing the residual sum of squares using partial derivatives, cementing your intuition for why the normal equations arise and how they connect to the matrix solution.

You will decompose the total variability in a response into explainable and unexplainable parts, and use the resulting $F$-statistic to test whether group means or regression predictors have a statistically significant effect.
